text: The surgeon uses a bipolar dissector to coagulate and cut the round ligament during a total laparoscopic cystectomy. The instrument is applied near the celiac artery with fifty watts of current.

The surgeon uses a bipolar dissector to coagulate and cut the infundibulopelvic ligament in a 50-year-old patient. Both ovaries and tubes are being removed. The instrument is applied at three points on the ligament before cutting between them. The broad ligament is visible with its two leaves open, and the bladder is not adherent due to previous vaginal deliveries.

The surgeon uses a cutter to separate the anterior peritoneum from the bladder, with the left-hand instrument holding the bladder peritoneum. The peritoneum is cut without coagulation, resulting in a bloodless separation with no visible capillary bleeding. The uterus is observed to be retroverted.
The surgeon uses a dissector to carefully peel off the posterior peritoneum from the underlying tissue, ensuring to remain above the arc of the uterosacral ligament to prevent avulsion and bleeding.

The surgeon encounters difficulty separating the central peritoneum fused with the posterior uterine wall. They move to the opposite side of the round ligament, coagulating at three points before cutting. Care is advised when handling the uterine pedicle, which can be coagulated using a simple bipolar, Grasper, or Maryland instrument.
The surgeon uses a bipolar dissector to coagulate and cut the IP ligament on the opposite side, efficiently progressing with the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon cuts a small vessel within the broad ligament, maintaining a lateral approach to avoid bleeding near the uterine part. The incision spans two to five centimeters of the broad ligament.

The surgeon separates the remaining part of the anterior peritoneum, followed by the dissection of the bladder. The procedure involves careful manipulation due to potential complications from previous cesarean sections or myomectomies. The surgeon encounters difficulty in separating the fused leaves of the broad ligament, which are coagulated to manage bleeding.

The surgeon uses a dissector to coagulate the uterine pedicle at multiple points, keeping the jaws open before pressing the pedal to ensure effective coagulation. The uterine pedicle is visible, with the left side being addressed while the right side remains intact, causing backflow. The field is managed to minimize bleeding and maintain clear vision.

The surgeon encounters the ovary and fallopian tube obstructing the surgical field during the posterior colpotomy. The uterine pedicle is visible, and the surgeon begins the colpotomy incision on a relatively small uterus. The initial cut is challenging, but once made, the tissue is easily manipulated and removed using a pipe.
The surgeon performs a left side lateral colpotomy using a bipolar cutter, demonstrating precise cutting and coagulation of the tissue.

The surgeon takes small bites of the vaginal wall during the post-colpotomy phase, ensuring proper coagulation and minimizing bleeding. The position is changed to enter through the right-hand side port for better access, using the cutter to continue the procedure.

The surgeon performs a smooth colpotomy using a detector instrument, adjusting the direction of the jaws as needed. The procedure minimizes gas leakage by reducing the need to frequently remove and reinsert the instrument, compared to other tools like bipolar seizure, ligature, or harmonic.

The surgeon removes the uterus after completing the colpotomy, using a Y-clid 1-0 suture with a round body needle to secure the posterior peritoneum and uterosacral ligaments. The assistant pushes the uterus slightly inside to help identify the margins, and the surgeon forms a knot using a double wrap followed by a one-one wrap technique.
The surgeon prepares for suturing by suctioning a large amount of blood from the pouch of Douglas (POD), revealing clear margins of the blood pool.

The assistant pulls the thread to lift the margins, facilitating easier suturing of the vaginal wall. The surgeon ensures both sides of the mucosa are properly included to prevent granulation formation.
The surgeon finishes suturing by creating a loop and tying a knot at one end, with the bladder visible on the anterior wall, making the suturing process more challenging.

The surgeon completes the third knot in the suture line, demonstrating the ease of the second layer compared to the first. The second layer of suturing is nearly finished, and the suture is terminated by tying a knot with the tail of the first knot. The surgeon performs a brief suctioning to clear the surgical field.
